title: Strait Of Hormuz: ହର୍ମୁଜ ପ୍ରଣାଳୀରେ ମାଲବାହୀ ଜାହାଜ ଉପରେ ଇରାନର ଆକ୍ରମଣ
description:
ତେହେରାନ: ବିଶ୍ବରେ ତୈଳ ପରିବହନ ପାଇଁ ଗୁରୁତ୍ବପୂର୍ଣ୍ଣ ହର୍ମୁଜ୍ ପ୍ରଣାଳୀରେ ପୁଣି ଥରେ ଗୁଳିଗୋଳା ବର୍ଷଣ ହୋଇଛି। ଏକ ବାଣିଜ୍ୟିକ ମାଲବାହୀ ଜାହାଜ ଉପରେ ଡ୍ରୋନ୍ ଆକ୍ରମଣ ପାଇଁ ଆମେରିକା ସିଧାସଳଖ ଇରାନର ରିଭଲ୍ୟୁସନାରୀ ଗାର୍ଡକୁ ଦାୟୀ କରିଛି। ଜାହାଜରୁ ଟୋଲ୍ ଆଦାୟ କରିବାକୁ ତେହେରାନର ଜିଦ୍ ଓ ଏହି ସଦ୍ୟତମ ଆକ୍ରମଣ ଆମେରିକା-ଇରାନ ଶାନ୍ତି ଚୁକ୍ତି ପାଇଁ ଏକ ଗମ୍ଭୀର ବିପଦ ସୃଷ୍ଟି କରିଛି। ଆମେରିକୀୟ ଅଧିକାରୀଙ୍କ ଅନୁଯାୟୀ, ସିଙ୍ଗାପୁର ପତାକା ବହନ କରୁଥିବା ‘ଏଭର ଲଭଲି’ ନାମକ କଣ୍ଟେନର ଜାହାଜକୁ ଓମାନ ଉପକୂଳରେ ଇରାନୀ ଅର୍ଦ୍ଧସାମରିକ ବାହିନୀର ଡ୍ରୋନ୍ ଦ୍ୱାରା ଟାର୍ଗେଟ କରାଯାଇଥିଲା।
ଶାନ୍ତି ପାଇଁ ଆହ୍ବାନ
ଆମେରିକା ଓ ଇରାନ ଶାନ୍ତି ଚୁକ୍ତି ସୂତାଖିଅରେ ଝୁଲୁଥିବାବେଳେ ଏହି ଆକ୍ରମଣ ଶାନ୍ତି ପାଇଁ ଏକ ଆହ୍ବାନ ବୋଲି କୁହାଯାଉଛି। ନ୍ୟୁଜ ଏଜେନ୍ସି ରଏଟର୍ସ ରିପୋର୍ଟ ଅନୁଯାୟୀ ଆମେରିକା ଅଧିକାରୀ କହିଛନ୍ତି ଯେ, ଆଇଆରଜିସି ଏହି ମାଲବାହୀ ଜାହାଜ ଉପରେ ଆକ୍ରମଣ କରିଛି। ବର୍ତ୍ତମାନ ଏହି ଆକ୍ରମଣ ପାଇଁ ଆମେରିକା ସରକାରଙ୍କ ପକ୍ଷରୁ କୌଣସି ପ୍ରତିକ୍ରିୟା ଆସିନାହିଁ। ଅନ୍ୟପକ୍ଷରେ ଇରାନର ଇସଲାମିକ ସରକାର ମଧ୍ୟ ଏହା ଉପରେ କୌଣସି ପ୍ରତିକ୍ରିୟା ଦେଇନାହିଁ। ତେବେ ଆମେରିକା ଓ ଇରାନ ମଧ୍ୟରେ ଚୁକ୍ତି ପରେ ଇରାନ ଚେତାବନୀ ଦେଇଥିଲା ଯେ, ଏହାର ବିନା ଅନୁମତିରେ ହର୍ମୁଜରେ ପ୍ରଣାଳୀ ଦେଇ ଯାଉଥିବା ଯେକୌଣସି ଜାହାଜ ଉପରେ କାର୍ଯ୍ୟାନୁଷ୍ଠାନ ଗ୍ରହଣ କରାଯିବ।
IB Chief: ମହେଶ ଦୀକ୍ଷିତ ଇଣ୍ଟେଲିଜେନ୍ସ ବ୍ୟୁରୋ ମୁଖ୍ୟ ନିଯୁକ୍ତ
ଏହି ଆକ୍ରମଣ ଉପରେ ବ୍ରିଟେନ ‌ନୌସେନା ପକ୍ଷରୁ ସୂଚନା ଦିଆଯାଇଛି ଯେ, ଓମାନ ଉପକୂଳ ନିକଟରେ ମାଲବାହକ ଜାହାଜ ସହିତ ଏକ ପ୍ରୋଜେକ୍ଟାଇଲ ମାଡ଼ ହୋଇଛି। ଏହାଦ୍ବାରା ଜାହାଜର କଣ୍ଟ୍ରୋଲ ରୁମ ନଷ୍ଟ ହୋଇଯାଇଥିଲା। ତେବେ ଏଥିରେ କୌଣସି ବ୍ୟକ୍ତିଙ୍କର ମୃତ୍ୟୁ ହୋଇନାହିଁ।

title: Ram Temple Case: All 8 Accused Arrested—What Each Of Them Was Responsible For
description: The Uttar Pradesh Police have arrested eight individuals involved in the alleged embezzlement of donations meant for the Ram Temple in Ayodhya.
content: The Uttar Pradesh Police on Thursday arrested all eight accused in the alleged Ram Temple donation embezzlement case, hours after registering the first FIR in connection with the alleged misappropriation of donations offered by devotees at the temple in Ayodhya. According to sources, all eight accused have been taken into custody and are most likely to be produced before a remand magistrate later on Friday.Police have begun questioning the accused to ascertain how the alleged embezzlement was carried out, the role of each individual, and since when the alleged misappropriation of devotees' donations had been taking place.Investigators are also trying to determine whether more people were involved in the alleged fraud and if the embezzlement was part of a larger organised racket. Further investigation is underway.Profiles of 8 accused named in the FIR The case was filed under multiple provisions of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS), including Sections 306, 316(5), 317(4), 317(5), 61, and 3(5), on the directions of the Uttar Pradesh government. The FIR names Anukalp Mishra, Lavkush Mishra, Avinash Shukla, Tinnu Yadav, Manish Yadav, and others.Ramshankar Yadav 'Tinnu': Formerly the driver for Trust General Secretary Champat Rai. He was directly involved in almost all temple operations. He was even found to be in possession of the keys to the donation boxes.Anukalp Mishra: An employee involved in counting cash. He has been identified as the mastermind behind the embezzlement case and was the first to be apprehended. A sum of ₹20 lakh was recovered from his residence in Kaushalpuri.Lavkush Mishra: Anukalp Mishra's brother-in-law. He was hired through the recommendation of his father-in-law, Ravindra Mishra (who is also Anukalp's father). Approximately ₹10 lakh in cash was found at his home.Manish Yadav: Tinnu Yadav's nephew. He had been hired by Tinnu only about four or five months prior. Some cash was also recovered from his premises.Subhash Srivastava: After retiring from Canara Bank, he was not only hired as a Trust employee but was also assigned to supervise the cash-counting process.Avinash Shukla: Involved in the counting process; a resident of Ayodhya city. A sum of ₹5 lakh was recovered from his account.Karunesh Pandey: Involved in the counting process; described as a close associate of Anukalp.Ramashankar Mishra: Involved in the counting of cash. Some cash was also recovered from his possession.This action follows allegations by former SP MLA from Ayodhya, Pawan Pandey, who claimed that between ₹7 crore and ₹7.5 crore in donations were misappropriated from the Ram Mandir. In response to these claims, the state government constituted a three-member Special Investigation Team (SIT) on June 14 to probe the alleged scam, acting on a request from the Shri Ram Janmabhoomi Teerth Kshetra Trust.Meanwhile, a fresh petition has been filed in the Supreme Court seeking a court-monitored investigation. (With agency inputs)

title: Ivory Coast Coach Accuses Bastian Schweinsteiger of Racist Remarks During FIFA W...
description: Emerse Fae, the head coach of Ivory Coast, has publicly criticised Bastian Schweinsteiger for his comments about African football teams during the FIFA World Cup 2026.
content: Ivory Coast head coach Emerse Fae has finally addressed and criticised former Germany footballer Bastian Schweinsteiger, accusing the commentator of employing outdated racial stereotypes to characterise African football teams competing in the ongoing edition of the FIFA World Cup 2026. Before Germany's group-stage encounter against Ivory Coast, Schweinsteiger cautioned viewers about what to expect from the West African powerhouse on the German television network ARD, which sparked the issue."A bit African football, a bit unorthodox, a bit wild, a bit perhaps also not so conditioned by tactics. We have to be prepared for it to be unpredictable," The 2014 World Cup winner said in his pre-game analysisEmerse Fae Criticises Bastian Schweinsteiger's RemarksFae picked the ideal time to finally address the controversial remarks. He led Ivory Coast to a convincing 2-0 victory over Curaçao on Thursday, thereby leading 'The Elephants' into the World Cup knockout stages for the first time in the history of their national side. Speaking following the momentous occasion, Fae insisted on highlighting the underlying bias even though he felt the remarks were merely awkward wording."We could call it racist, if we were calling a spade a spade," Faé told reporters. "When I heard his comment, I was disappointed. Disappointed in the man. It is odd he would speak that way. I wonder if he was just trying to create a buzz for his broadcast career."Fae stressed that his team uses great tactical intelligence, careful preparation, and astute strategic discipline to win games instead of relying solely on raw, uncoordinated physical attributes.Schweinsteiger Faces BacklashSchweinsteiger's comments have already drawn harsh criticism in Germany for reinforcing stereotypes from the colonial past. The comment was criticised by media analyst Patrick Schnitzler on Instagram as an illustration of "racist prejudices that we are all passing on unnoticed."Prominent journalist Philipp Awounou wrote in Der Spiegel magazine that although he did not think the former Bayern Munich star was personally malicious, the language used directly contributed to deeply flawed colonial-era stereotypes that portray African athletes as chaotic or lacking in mental discipline.

title: Angelina Jolie Opens Up About Drawing From Personal Pain For 'Couture' | N18G
description: Angelina Jolie is opening up about how her own life experiences helped in shaping her performance in the upcoming drama 'Couture'. The Oscar-winning actor, who underwent a preventive mastectomy in 2013 after discovering a gene mutation that increased her risk of breast cancer, plays a filmmaker grappling with a breast cancer diagnosis in the film. Check out the video to find out more.

title: Nothing Phone 4b Key Specifications Tipped; May Get 120Hz Screen, 50-Megapixel Camera
description: The upcoming handset was recently teased in a blue colourway
content: Nothing Phone 4b is scheduled to launch as the first handset in the new “b” series next month. The company recently showcased the design of the phone, while also revealing a blue colourway. While details still remain under wraps, a tipster has leaked key specifications of the upcoming handset. It is tipped to sport a 6.7-inch 120Hz display. The Nothing Phone 4b could be powered by a 6-series processor from Qualcomm, backed by a battery with a capacity exceeding the 5,000mAh mark.

title: 'Kabhi-kabhi lagta tha Bombay chhodna padega...': Raakh actor Ramandeep Yadav on financial struggles, survival, rejection, loneliness and how Rajjo changed his life | ET NOW EXCLUSIVE
description: Raakh actor Ramandeep Yadav opens up about almost leaving Mumbai due to financial struggles, surviving rejection, battling loneliness, his family's sacrifices, and how OTT transformed his career, turning Rajjo into his breakthrough role and changing his life forever.
content: There are performances that leave audiences talking, and then there are performances that completely transform the perception of an actor. For Ramandeep Yadav, Raakh has become that defining moment. As Rajjo, one of the series' most feared yet emotionally conflicted characters, the actor has earned widespread praise for portraying a man shaped by violence, survival and circumstance rather than one-dimensional evil. The role marks another significant milestone for Ramandeep after projects such as Manmarziyaan and CAT, showcasing his ability to disappear into layered, psychologically complex characters. Behind the intensity on screen, however, lies years of theatre training, a childhood devoted to cricket, countless auditions, financial uncertainty and a relentless determination to survive in Mumbai long enough for the right opportunity to arrive.In this exclusive conversation with ET Now, Ramandeep opens up like never before about the emotional toll of becoming Rajjo, the haunting scene that stayed with him long after filming ended, and how director Prosit Roy Roy encouraged him to truly experience emotions instead of merely performing them. He also speaks candidly about almost leaving Mumbai because of financial struggles, battling loneliness and self-doubt, why he credits OTT for democratising opportunities for actors, and the sacrifices his family made so he could pursue his dream. From revealing how Rajjo's survival instinct was inspired by a cockroach to explaining why he refuses to describe his journey as a "struggle", the actor reflects on the lessons that shaped both his career and the person behind one of the year's most compelling performances. Excerpts from the interview:Q: Rajjo is being described as one of the most feared characters in Raakh. What was your first reaction when the role came your way? A: My first reaction was honestly one of excitement. Mera pehla reaction yahi tha ki yaar, Raman ko pata tha ki mujhe lead role ke liye test kar rahe hain. For any actor who comes to Mumbai, getting an audition for one of the lead characters is a huge opportunity. When you move here, you always dream that one day you'll get a substantial role—something that truly allows you to perform. So, for me, the pressure wasn't about playing Rajjo. The pressure came from the fact that I'd finally received an opportunity like this, and I didn't want to waste it. Maine tabhi decide kar liya tha ki agar yeh karna hai, toh all in jaaunga. I couldn't approach the character superficially. I knew I had to dive deep into his psychology and understand every layer of who he was.            Q: Negative characters often run the risk of becoming one-dimensional villains. How did you ensure Rajjo felt real and human? A: For me, Rajjo always represented hope. Mujhe Rajjo mein ek hope dikhai deti hai, aur shayad audience ko bhi wohi feel hota hai. Throughout the story, you feel that he might stop Babu, save the child, or eventually choose the right path. That possibility exists because Rajjo begins with a certain innocence. That's what fascinated me the most. Rajjo isn't someone who's brutally evil from the beginning. Unlike Babu, who is consistently ruthless, Rajjo gradually transforms into a criminal. Woh bante-bante criminal banta hai. That journey reflects how circumstances, society and personal experiences can shape a person's psychology. I felt there was tremendous scope to humanise him rather than present him as just another violent villain. The character's graph really excited me. He changes in very subtle ways. At times, you feel he won't do anything wrong, and then suddenly he surprises you with an unexpected action. That unpredictability made him incredibly interesting to perform. Q: Rajjo's transformation feels psychologically layered. How did you build that arc? A: A lot of it came from understanding where he came from. Rajjo belongs to Haryana, and he's grown up in a deeply male-dominated environment where masculinity is constantly asserted. Socho uska bachpan kitna brutal raha hoga. Kis wajah se usne Haryana chhoda hoga? Those were the questions we kept asking ourselves. Many of these details weren't written in the script. We created them during our preparation. The script only hinted at his past, so we built an entire emotional backstory—how he may have met Pyare Mohan, what kind of experiences shaped him, and what eventually pushed him towards crime. During the acting workshops, we also explored Rajjo's animal instinct. The script had already established that survival was his biggest motivation. So we asked ourselves, agar Rajjo kisi jaanwar jaisa hota, toh kaunsa hota? We eventually chose a cockroach because it symbolises survival. No matter what happens, it always finds a way to stay alive. That instinct became central to Rajjo's behaviour. He's someone who often goes unnoticed in a crowd. Usse validation chahiye. Woh keh raha hota hai, "Main bhi yahan hoon, mujhe bhi notice karo." Babu becomes the first person who gives him that validation, which is why Rajjo follows him. But his loyalty only lasts until his own survival is threatened. The moment it becomes a question of saving himself, he's willing to distance himself even from Babu. Yeh bahut human tendency hai. Jab survival ki baat aati hai, insaan bahut kuch kar sakta hai. That's what makes Rajjo believable rather than simply evil. Q: As Rajjo becomes more violent, what changes within him? A: For me, it was like someone experiencing power for the very first time. Jaise kisi jaanwar ke muh ko pehli baar khoon lag jaata hai. Rajjo has never experienced that feeling of dominance or control before. The moment he does, he completely loses himself in it. He's no longer thinking rationally. He's simply carried away by the adrenaline. Especially during the Agra portions, you can see that he's actually enjoying the violence because it's an entirely new sensation for him. But eventually, when the verdict scene arrives, everything catches up with him. That's when he truly realises the gravity of everything he has done. Unlike Babu, Rajjo isn't entirely devoid of humanity. Mere hisaab se Rajjo galat kaam karte hue bhi sochta hai ki kam galat kya ho sakta hai. For example, in the final sequence with Sahil, when he realises Sahil is still alive, he chooses to end his suffering quickly instead of prolonging it. If it had been Babu, he might have left him there to suffer. That's the fundamental difference between the two characters. Rajjo still retains fragments of humanity, even after everything he's done. Q: Was there a particular scene in Raakh that emotionally drained you or stayed with you long after the shoot? A: Yes, there was one scene that still stays with me. It's the sequence where Rajjo strikes Sahil with the sword for the first time after Babu orders him to do it. Aaj bhi kabhi-kabhi main uss scene ke baare mein sochta hoon aur lagta hai, "Yaar, woh tha kya? Mere saath uss waqt hua kya tha?" We were shooting the sequence on a digital set with a green screen. Initially, I assumed we would film it in smaller portions because the storyboard had been broken down into multiple shots. But our director said, "Nahi, isse one-take approach mein karte hain." I was surprised because it was an emotionally and physically demanding sequence. Eventually, we performed it over four takes. By the end of it, I wasn't really present on the set anymore. Main poori tarah Rajjo ke trance mein chala gaya tha. While performing that scene, I genuinely felt as though I had killed someone. Repeating it four times took an emotional toll on me. The shiver I experienced during that performance is something I still remember. If you watch the scene carefully, you'll probably notice it in Rajjo's eyes. Those emotions weren't manufactured—they were completely real in that moment. Even today, I believe it was one of the most difficult scenes I've ever performed.            Q: How did you prepare mentally to step into the mind of someone as dangerous as Rajjo? A: From the very beginning, I knew I wanted Rajjo to have a certain presence. Meri sirf ek hi wish thi—jab Rajjo kisi ke saamne khada ho, toh saamne wala aadmi andar se thoda sa kaamp jaaye. I wanted people to feel his intensity even before he spoke. Coming from a theatre background really helped because theatre teaches you to build a character from the ground up. We don't just learn the lines—we create an entire life for the character. I spent a great deal of time working on Rajjo's body language, his dialect, the way he walked, how he stood, and even the kind of music he might listen to. Main yeh bhi sochta tha ki agar Rajjo ko free time mile, toh woh karta kya hoga? Woh kis tarah ki zindagi jeeta hoga? These details may never appear on screen, but they help you understand the character from within. During the workshops, we also explored Rajjo's psychology. Why does he remain loyal to Babu? What drives his decisions? What does fear mean to him? Humne uski poori psychology ko explore kiya. One of the biggest discoveries during the process was Rajjo's animal instinct. We associated him with a cockroach because, for us, it symbolised survival. Woh kisi bhi situation mein zinda rehna chahta hai. Survival ke liye woh kuch bhi kar sakta hai. To stay connected to the character throughout the shoot, I even created a mood board. It included photographs of Vinod Khanna because Rajjo once dreamt of becoming an actor. There were images of Amitabh Bachchan, references to the Emergency-era forced sterilisation campaign that shaped Rajjo's backstory, and several photographs of cockroaches in different situations to remind me of his survival instinct. Yeh mood board mere liye sirf references nahi tha. Yeh Rajjo ka poora emotional map tha. Whenever I looked at it, I could instantly reconnect with his emotional journey and understand where he was mentally in every scene. Q: You mentioned that Rajjo still retains some humanity despite everything he does. How did you portray that in the final scenes? A: I wanted that humanity to remain visible, even in Rajjo's darkest moments. Take the sequence where Rajjo goes to dispose of Sahil's body. As I mentioned earlier, when he suddenly realises Sahil is still alive, he strikes him again. From the outside, it may simply look like another act of violence. But from Rajjo's perspective, it's very different. Uske dimaag mein sirf ek hi baat chal rahi hoti hai—agar maarna hi hai, toh ek hi baar mein khatam kar do. Usse aur dard na ho. He's not trying to torture Sahil or prolong his suffering. In Rajjo's twisted understanding, he's putting an end to it quickly. That's where I felt Rajjo differed from Babu. He is deeply flawed and commits terrible crimes, but somewhere inside him, fragments of humanity still survive. Wahi contradiction mujhe Rajjo ke character mein sabse zyada interesting lagti thi. Q: Rajjo often seems to choose the lesser evil. Was that something you consciously built into the character? A: Absolutely. Even in Rajjo's darkest moments, I wanted him to retain a small sense of humanity. Take the scene where he goes to dispose of Sahil's body. When Rajjo realises Sahil is still alive, he strikes him again. On the surface, it appears to be another act of brutality. But from Rajjo's perspective, it's almost an act of mercy. Uske dimaag mein sirf ek hi baat chal rahi hoti hai—agar maarna hi hai, toh ek hi baar mein khatam kar do. Usse aur takleef kyun deni? If it had been Babu, he would probably have left Sahil there to suffer. Rajjo doesn't think that way. Even while doing something horrific, somewhere inside he's still trying to choose what he believes is the lesser wrong. I know the audience may not consciously notice that layer, but as an actor, I wanted to understand Rajjo's psychology right until the very end. Q: During the verdict sequence, did you discover something new about Rajjo? A: Yes, actually. I still remember that during the verdict scene, Ali sir asked me, "If Rajjo had to choose how he dies, what would that look like?" I hadn't really thought about it before, but the first thought that came to my mind was that Rajjo would want to drown. There's an earlier line in the series where Rajjo says that life in his village was peaceful and that he would spend hours in the pond. In the backstory I had created for him, that pond became his safe space. It was the one place where he found peace and truly felt like himself. Toh mujhe laga ki itne guilt ke baad, Rajjo apni safe space mein hi marna chahega. That was the moment I realised I had completely started thinking like Rajjo rather than as myself. Q: Was there any aspect of Rajjo's personality that you personally struggled to understand or justify? A: Yes. One thing I kept questioning was Rajjo's relationship with Babu. Every time Babu simply called out his name, Rajjo would immediately stop and listen. Initially, I felt that survival alone wasn't enough to explain that connection. Validation wali baat thi, lekin mujhe lagta tha sirf validation itna strong reason nahi ho sakta. I remember one of our producers asking me, "What exactly is there between Rajjo and Babu? Is it love?" Honestly, I didn't have a clear answer. Instead of trying to define their relationship with a label, I realised that Akash and I first needed to build that connection off-screen. So, I consciously spent more time getting to know him. I wanted us to genuinely trust and understand each other. The more comfortable we became, the more naturally that relationship began to appear on screen. It's difficult to describe their bond in a single word. Log usse pyaar bol sakte hain, lekin mere liye woh usse bhi zyada complicated hai. Sometimes there are relationships that can't be explained—they're simply felt. That's what I wanted between Rajjo and Babu: an unspoken understanding that they complete each other in a strange way. Q: What was it like sharing screen space with actors like Ali Fazal, Sonali Bendre and Aamir Bashir? A: It was an incredible experience. They're all such accomplished actors, but what surprised me the most was how humble they are. Itne bade stars hone ke baad bhi unhone kabhi humein junior feel nahi karwaya. As actors, we often create an image of stars in our minds. But when you actually work with them, you realise how warm, generous and approachable they are. They make you feel so comfortable that everything begins to flow naturally. One of the biggest lessons I learnt from them was that success and humility can absolutely go hand in hand. Main bhi future mein agar kuch achieve karun, toh unki tarah hi grounded rehna chahta hoon. Q: What did director Prosit Roy  Roy encourage you to explore as an actor that you hadn't attempted before? A: The biggest gift Prosit Roy  sir gave me was trust. Initially, I wondered whether I'd even get the opportunity to discuss my ideas because he had already been developing the project for months before I joined. But from day one, he gave me complete creative freedom. Hum dono ke beech constant communication hoti thi. Main Rajjo ke baare mein jo bhi sochta tha, woh patiently sunte the. For an actor, that's incredibly valuable. When the actor and director are completely in sync, there are no doubts left while performing. You're simply living the character. Prosit Roy  sir never treated it as his project. He always made us feel as though we were building Rajjo together. He also pushed me emotionally. There were moments when he didn't just want me to perform an emotion—he wanted me to genuinely experience it. He even shared playlists that helped me enter Rajjo's emotional state before difficult scenes. Method acting ka credit woh kabhi khud nahi lete, lekin main hamesha unhe uska credit deta hoon. Of course, Rajjo wasn't created by one person alone. Anusha, Sandeep's writing, Ayush Trivedi's dialogues, the cinematography, the background score, the costume department, the make-up artists and the sound team—everyone contributed to shaping the character. I even started wearing Rajjo's ring during script readings. Whenever I wore that ring, I instantly felt like Rajjo. Aur jaise hi script band hoti thi, main woh ring utaar deta tha. These small rituals helped me switch into the character with ease. One thing that really impressed me was our use of sync sound. Because very little dubbing was required, most of the emotions audiences see on screen are exactly what we experienced on the day of the shoot. Emotion ko chhe mahine baad dubbing mein recreate karna bahut mushkil hota hai. I'm grateful we didn't have to rely on that. The entire team gave more than 100 per cent, and I think that's one of the biggest reasons Raakh feels as authentic as it does.           Q: What did director Prosit Roy push you to explore as an actor that you hadn't attempted before? A: The biggest thing Prosit Roy  sir gave me was trust. As an actor, that kind of support means everything. Jab director aap par itna trust dikhata hai na, toh woh apne aap mein bahut badi responsibility ban jaati hai. He's such a brilliant filmmaker that he can elevate your performance simply through the way he designs a scene—the framing, the camera angles and the visual language. Even if I perform a scene in a certain way, he knows exactly how to present it so that it becomes even more impactful on screen. But he never wanted me to merely perform an emotion. He wanted me to genuinely experience it. That's why he would even share playlists with me before certain scenes. Music became a way of helping me enter Rajjo's emotional world. It's part of his process, although he never takes credit for it. Method acting ka credit woh kabhi khud nahi lete, lekin main hamesha unhe uska credit deta hoon. Unhone mujhe bahut help ki hai. At the same time, Rajjo wasn't created by one person alone—it was the result of everyone's collective effort. Anusha, Sandeep wrote a fantastic script and also directed two episodes, while Ayush Trivedi's dialogues added tremendous depth to the character. One of my favourite moments is Rajjo's dialogue where he says that if someone has to die, let it be him—but not the pigeon. That single line captures Rajjo's entire emotional journey. Even after becoming brutal, there's still a small part of him that feels deeply. It reflects his loneliness and his belief that, in the end, no one truly belongs to anyone. Wahi ek chhota sa moment Rajjo ko sirf villain nahi, insaan banata hai. The show became what it is because every department worked with complete dedication. Everyone gave far more than 100 per cent. I remember we were shooting the hut sequence during a storm. The costume team could easily have stayed back at the base, but instead they were out there holding the hut so that we could continue filming. The production team, the costume department—everyone stepped in wherever they were needed. Sab log apna department bhool kar sirf show ke liye kaam kar rahe the. Woh dekhkar bahut accha laga. I'd also like to acknowledge our sync sound recordist, Ravi Desar. He's one of the finest technicians I've worked with. Because we shot with sync sound, I had to do very little dubbing. That's a huge advantage because emotions are always at their most authentic in the moment. Chhe mahine ya ek saal baad dubbing mein wahi emotion wapas laana bahut mushkil hota hai. I'm genuinely grateful that most of what audiences see is exactly what we performed on set. Q: Before acting, you represented Chandigarh in cricket. Was cricket once a more realistic career than cinema? A: Absolutely. To be honest, cinema was never part of my plan. Acting toh kabhi plan hi nahi tha. When I was playing cricket, I wasn't even thinking about representing India. I didn't have a long-term roadmap. I was simply focused on becoming better every single day. I believe there's a difference between having a plan and being completely committed to your work. Plan hona alag baat hai, lekin focus hona usse bhi zyada zaroori hai. Things didn't unfold the way I had imagined, but I truly believe God had something better planned for me. That's how I eventually found acting. Today, I'm fortunate to be part of a show like Raakh, which has connected with audiences across the world. Main bahut strongly maanta hoon ki jo bhi hota hai, achhe ke liye hota hai. Q: In today's era of social media fame, do theatre-trained actors still have an advantage? A: I think a strong foundation always helps, no matter which profession you're in. People who come from theatre usually have tremendous faith in their craft because they've spent years understanding the fundamentals of performance. I've worked hard on my craft too, but cinema is a completely different medium. You can't assume that because someone is a good theatre actor, they'll automatically excel in front of the camera. Camera ki apni language hoti hai. Usko samajhna bahut zaroori hai. One of the biggest adjustments for theatre actors is that theatre encourages larger physical expressions, whereas the camera notices the smallest movement. The more truthful and restrained your performance is, the more effective it looks on screen. If you overplay even slightly, it can appear exaggerated. Camera har chhoti cheez observe karta hai. Isliye jitna natural rahoge, utna hi performance strong lagega. Every medium has its own grammar, and an actor has to respect that. But yes, theatre has helped me immensely. It gave me discipline, patience and the ability to build a character from scratch. I'll always be grateful for that. Q: What is something theatre taught you that no acting school or workshop can? A: I honestly can't compare because I've never been to an acting school. Main acting school kabhi gaya hi nahi, toh uske baare mein comment karna fair nahi hoga. Ho sakta hai agar gaya hota toh meri soch alag hoti. But what theatre definitely gave me is something much bigger than acting—it made me a better human being. I believe every form of art makes you more aware of society. It teaches you to observe, empathise and genuinely understand human emotions. Theatre ne mujhe sirf acting nahi sikhayi, usne mujhe insaan banna sikhaya. In our everyday lives, we're all caught up in routines. We wake up, follow a schedule and rarely stop to observe what's happening around us. But as artists, that's our responsibility. Our job is to absorb life and then reflect it through our work. If your senses aren't active, you can't truthfully portray human emotions or society. Theatre trained me to keep my eyes, ears and heart open all the time. Main logon ko observe karta hoon, unki body language dekhta hoon, unke emotions samajhne ki koshish karta hoon. Theatre ne mujhe yeh aadat di. I'm extremely grateful for that because it has changed me as a person. I truly believe what Zakir Khan sir often says—that your work can be 19 out of 20, but as a human being, you should strive to be a complete 20. That's something I constantly remind myself. Achha actor banna zaroori hai, lekin usse pehle achha insaan banna aur bhi zaroori hai. Q: From Manmarziyaan to CAT and now Raakh, what has changed the most in your approach to acting? A: Every project has taught me something different. In Manmarziyaan, I only had one scene and received the script almost on the spot. There wasn't much time to prepare. Then came CAT, where I got the complete script. For the first time, I understood the entire story and knew exactly where my character stood within that world. But even then, although it was an important role, the narrative wasn't centred entirely around my character. Raakh was a completely different experience. In theatre, when you're playing the lead, the entire story revolves around you. Even when you're not on stage, your character's presence continues to influence the narrative. The same applies to Rajjo and Babu. Even when they're not physically present in a scene, people are talking about them. As an audience, you continue to feel their presence. Yeh bahut interesting process tha kyunki character screen par na hoke bhi kahaani mein zinda rehta hai. Leading a project brings a completely different level of responsibility. You're carrying an entire show on your shoulders. Writers spend years researching and developing these characters, while producers invest enormous faith and resources into the project. Toh naturally aapko lagta hai ki unki mehnat ko justify karna meri responsibility hai. As your career grows, that responsibility only becomes greater. Whether it's a single scene or an entire series, your director expects you to give your absolute best. At the same time, every additional day on set teaches you something new. Jitna zyada set par waqt bitaoge, utna hi zyada actor ke taur par grow karoge. Q: You have often spoken about how collaborative filmmaking is. Could you elaborate on that? A: Absolutely. People often appreciate the actor they see on screen, but what ultimately reaches the audience is the result of hundreds of people working together. The director, the cinematographer, the editors, the background score, the make-up artists, the costume department—everyone contributes to shaping a performance. For example, every day during make-up, the team would recreate Rajjo's scars and marks. The moment they finished, I immediately felt like I was back inside the character. Jaise hi woh marks lagte the, mujhe lagta tha—haan, Rajjo wapas aa gaya. There was also a ring that Rajjo wore in the series. I borrowed an extra one from the costume department and started wearing it even while reading the script. Whenever I was preparing for Rajjo, I wore that ring. The moment I stopped working on the character, I took it off. Yeh chhoti-chhoti rituals bahut help karti hain character ke zone mein jaane ke liye. Prosit Roy sir encouraged all of us to discover these little personal rituals. He pushed me emotionally in ways I hadn't experienced before. Unhone kabhi sirf performance nahi maangi. Woh chahte the ki main woh emotion sach mein feel karun. That's a huge responsibility because when your director trusts you so completely, you naturally want to give your very best. Q: Before acting, you represented Chandigarh in cricket tournaments. Was there ever a point when cricket felt like a more realistic career than cinema? A: Always. To be honest, cinema was never part of my plan. Acting toh kabhi option hi nahi tha. When I was playing cricket, I wasn't even thinking about representing India. I never had a long-term plan. I was simply focused on the work I was doing at that moment. There's a difference between having a plan and being completely focused. Plan banana alag baat hai, lekin apne kaam par poora focus rakhna usse bhi zyada zaroori hai. That's how I approached cricket. I gave it everything I had. Things eventually didn't work out the way I had imagined, but I truly believe God had something else planned for me. That's how acting entered my life. Today, I'm fortunate to be part of a show like Raakh, which is being appreciated not just in India but across the world. Seeing audiences connect with Rajjo has been incredibly rewarding. Main bahut strongly maanta hoon ki jo bhi hota hai, achhe ke liye hota hai. Shayad uparwale ne mere liye yahi raasta likha tha. Q: In an era dominated by social media fame, do you think theatre-trained actors still have an edge? A: I think having a strong foundation always gives you an advantage, regardless of the profession you're in. People who come from theatre usually have immense faith in their craft because they've spent years understanding the fundamentals of performance. Theatre aapko shortcut nahi deta. Woh aapko process sikhata hai. That said, theatre doesn't automatically make you a great screen actor. When you step in front of the camera, you're entering a completely different medium, and you have to respect its grammar. The biggest adjustment for theatre actors is that theatre encourages larger physical expressions, while the camera captures even the smallest detail. Camera har chhoti si movement pakad leta hai. The more truthful and natural your performance is, the better it looks on screen. If you overplay your emotions even slightly, it can appear exaggerated. That's why understanding the medium is extremely important. Har medium ki apni language aur apni boundaries hoti hain. Agar aap unhe samajh jaate ho, tabhi aap us medium mein achha perform kar sakte ho. But yes, theatre has helped me immensely, and I'll always be grateful for that foundation. Q: What is something theatre taught you that no acting school or workshop can? A: I honestly can't compare because I've never been to an acting school. Main acting school kabhi gaya hi nahi, isliye uske baare mein opinion dena sahi nahi hoga. Ho sakta hai agar gaya hota toh meri soch kuch aur hoti. But one thing theatre definitely gave me was humanity. Theatre doesn't just teach you how to perform—it teaches you how to observe life. It makes you more aware of society and helps you understand people, emotions and relationships. Theatre ne mujhe sirf actor nahi banaya, usne mujhe insaan banna sikhaya. In our daily lives, most of us are trapped in routines. We wake up, follow our schedules and rarely stop to notice what's happening around us. But as artists, that's exactly what we're supposed to do. Our responsibility is to observe society, understand people and then reflect those experiences through our work. That's why it's important for an artist to keep all their senses constantly active. Log kaise chalte hain, kaise baat karte hain, kaise react karte hain—yeh sab observe karna theatre ne hi sikhaya. I'm extremely grateful for that because it has changed me as a person. As Zakir Khan sir often says, your work can be 19 out of 20, but as a human being, you should strive to become a complete 20. I remind myself of that every day. Achha actor banna zaroori hai, lekin usse pehle achha insaan banna aur bhi zaroori hai. Main hamesha usi direction mein grow karne ki koshish karta hoon. Q: From Manmarziyaan to CAT and now Raakh, what has changed the most in your approach to acting? A: Every project has taught me something different, and I think that's the biggest blessing an actor can ask for. With Manmarziyaan, I had only one scene. I received the script on the spot, so there wasn't much time to prepare or understand the larger world of the story. Wahan bas scene samajhna tha aur perform karna tha. Then came CAT, where I received the complete script for the first time. I understood the entire narrative, knew where my character stood, and how he contributed to the story. That itself was a huge learning experience. But Raakh was completely different. Although my character in CAT was important, the story didn't revolve around him. In Raakh, Rajjo is one of the central characters, and that naturally changes your responsibility as an actor. Coming from theatre also helped me understand that. When you're playing the lead in a play, you're connected to every scene—even when you're not physically on stage. The story continues to revolve around your character. The same applies to Rajjo and Babu. Even when they're not in the frame, people are talking about them. As an audience, you continue to feel their presence. Character screen par na ho, phir bhi uski presence mehsoos honi chahiye. Mere liye wahi strong writing aur strong performance ki nishaani hai. Leading a project is a completely different journey. You're carrying an entire show on your shoulders. The writers have spent years researching and developing these characters, the producers have invested enormous faith in the project, and the director trusts you with bringing them to life. Toh naturally responsibility bhi utni hi badh jaati hai. As your career progresses, you'll receive bigger opportunities, but you'll also have to shoulder greater responsibility. Even if it's just a single scene, your director expects you to give your absolute best. At the same time, the more time you spend on a set, the more you grow as an actor. Jitna zyada aap character ke saath waqt bitaate ho, utna hi woh character aapko kuch naya sikha deta hai. Going forward, I want to play characters that genuinely matter to the story. I want to portray people who have their own emotional graph, their own voice and their own perspective. Main aise characters nahi karna chahta jo sirf screen fill karne ke liye ho. Agar character ko kahaani se hata do aur story par koi farq hi na pade, toh phir us character ka purpose kya hai? Rajjo is important because if you remove him from the story, the entire narrative changes. Those are exactly the kind of roles I want to keep doing. Q: Was there ever a phase when you doubted whether you would make it in the industry? A: Honestly, I never doubted myself as an actor. Apni acting ability par mujhe kabhi doubt nahi hua. What I did struggle with was survival. Living in Mumbai isn't easy. You have rent to pay, bills to manage and everyday expenses. Sometimes, surviving in the city itself becomes the biggest challenge. There were moments when I genuinely felt I might have to leave Mumbai—not because I had lost confidence in my craft, but because things had become financially difficult. Kabhi-kabhi lagta tha ki shayad kuch time ke liye Bombay chhodna pade. I still remember that after auditioning for Raakh, I went back to Chandigarh for around ten days. At that point, I had almost convinced myself that if nothing worked out, I'd pack my bags and return. Mentally, I had reached that stage. But then life surprised me. God had different plans, and today I'm back in Mumbai, talking about Raakh. Main maanta hoon ki uparwala jab darwaza band karta hai, toh kahin aur ek naya darwaza khol bhi deta hai. So no, I never lost faith in myself. I only worried about surviving long enough to receive the right opportunity. Q: OTT has created opportunities beyond traditional hero roles. Do you think it has made the industry more democratic? A: Absolutely. I always explain it through a sports analogy because that's the world I come from. For me, OTT is like the IPL. If you perform well in the IPL, selectors notice you and you get the opportunity to represent India. Similarly, OTT gives actors a platform where their work can reach audiences directly, without unnecessary barriers. OTT kehta hai, "Aao, perform karo. Agar kaam achha hai, log khud dekh lenge." That's a beautiful thing. Cinema involves massive investments, so naturally producers look for credibility before taking risks. OTT has created a space where actors can build that credibility. For artists like us, that's incredibly important. You're interviewing me today largely because people discovered me through OTT. People watched CAT, and that's one of the reasons I was considered for Raakh. Tomorrow, because of Raakh, someone might consider me for a leading role in a feature film. Yeh chain isi tarah banti hai. Ek project doosre project ka raasta kholta hai. OTT hasn't just created opportunities for actors. It's created employment for writers, directors, technicians, editors, cinematographers, costume designers and hundreds of crew members. Bahut saare logon ki rozi-roti is ecosystem se chal rahi hai. I'm genuinely grateful for that. Another advantage is the space it gives storytelling. A feature film runs for two or three hours, whereas a web series can unfold over seven or eight hours. That gives writers and actors enough time to properly build their characters. Character ko saans lene ki jagah milti hai. Many scenes that would otherwise be cut from a film can remain in a series, allowing audiences to connect more deeply with the emotional journey. The reach is incredible too. I remember travelling on the Delhi Metro when I noticed someone watching Raakh on their phone. Main sach mein ruk gaya aur socha—yaar, log waqai show dekh rahe hain. That moment made me realise how far the show had travelled. Today, people from different countries are watching it, reviewing it and discussing it online. Seeing Raakh trend globally among non-English shows is a huge achievement for the entire team. But for us, this is only the beginning. Abhi toh journey shuru hui hai. Main bas yahi chahta hoon ki aur achha kaam karta rahoon aur Raakh ko aur zyada logon tak pahunchaaun.                 https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RdB_TgwLPqsQ: What kind of home did you grow up in? Were films and acting ever a part of family conversations? A: Not at all. Cinema was never something we seriously discussed at home. I come from a sports-oriented family. My elder brother was into sports, my sister played hockey and is now a Physical Education teacher. As a child, I also played hockey before switching to cricket around Class 6 or 7. Pata hi nahi chala hockey kab chhoot gayi aur cricket kab zindagi ka hissa ban gaya. We all loved sports, and that's what our conversations revolved around. Like every Indian family, we watched television together. From around 7.30 in the evening until 11 at night, everyone would gather in front of the TV. It was family time. TV dekhna sirf entertainment tha. Hum audience ki tarah enjoy karte the. But it was never as though I watched films and thought, "One day I'll become an actor." There was no such atmosphere or ambition in our house. Acting ka koi sapna bachpan se nahi tha. Bas zindagi apne flow mein chal rahi thi. Q: What's the one sacrifice your family made for your career that you can never repay? A: Without a doubt, it's my elder brother's sacrifice. He's the eldest in the family, and I've always believed that the eldest child ends up carrying responsibilities much earlier than everyone else. He's an incredibly talented person and deserves so much more for himself, but life handed him responsibilities instead. Unhone apni zindagi se zyada hamesha hum sab ke baare mein socha. Because he took those responsibilities on his shoulders, I got the freedom to chase my dreams. My sister could build her own career, and I could come to Mumbai to become an actor. My father works in the education department, and my parents also made enormous sacrifices. They left their village and moved to the city to build a better future for us. It wasn't an easy decision. Apna gaon, apne log, apni poori zindagi chhodkar naye shehar mein shuruat karna bahut himmat ki baat hoti hai. I was born in Chandigarh, but my brother and sister were born in the village. My parents broke that generational cycle so that we could have better opportunities. I'll never be able to repay that. Main jo aaj kar paa raha hoon, woh sirf unki wajah se hai. Q: Is there a childhood memory that reminds you of everything your family did for you? A: There are many. I remember a phase when we were living in Chandigarh while my mother had to stay back in the village because of certain family responsibilities. I was probably in Class 9 or 10 at the time. During that period, my father managed everything at home. He would wake up early, cook breakfast and make sure all of us were ready for the day. Everything at home functioned as a team effort. Subah uthkar hum decide karte the ki kaun sabzi kaatega, kaun aata goondhega aur kaun paani ki bottles bharega. Everyone had a fixed responsibility. My father went to work, my sister had college and sports, and I had school followed by cricket practice. Our day started at five in the morning. We'd go to practice, return home, quickly get ready for school, come back in the afternoon, cook, eat and then head back to the academy. Uss waqt thakaan ka time hi nahi hota tha. Bas routine chalta rehta tha. My sister would cycle me to my cricket ground before going to her own practice. My brother would often drop me to the academy on his bicycle and sometimes stay back to watch my entire match. I used to feel awkward and tell him to go home. Main kehta tha, "Bhai, aap jao na." Lekin woh kehte the, "Nahi, main dekhunga." Today, when I look back, I realise how many silent sacrifices they made without ever talking about them. Those are things I'll never be able to repay. Q: Many actors describe their journey as a struggle. Do you see it that way? A: Honestly, I don't like calling it a struggle. I prefer calling it a process. Main struggle shabd use hi nahi karta. The reason is simple. When you say your journey was only about struggle, you unintentionally overlook all the people who stood beside you and gave their best for you. My family, my friends and so many others supported me wholeheartedly. Unhone apna 100 per cent diya. Agar main usse sirf struggle bol doon, toh unki mehnat ki value kam ho jaati hai. Life naturally has its ups and downs. Sometimes things work out exactly the way you want, and sometimes they don't. But every phase teaches you something. I've been in Mumbai for around four years now, and I've learnt one important lesson—you should always prepare for the worst while continuing to believe in the best. Main hamesha worst-case scenario ke liye mentally ready rehta hoon, lekin umeed kabhi nahi chhodta. I genuinely believe God has a timing for everything. When the right time comes, opportunities begin to fall into place. Bas patience rakhna padta hai. Uparwala kabhi galat waqt par kuch nahi deta.                    View this post on Instagram            A post shared by prime video IN (@primevideoin)Q: Have you ever struggled with anxiety, loneliness or self-doubt during your journey? A: Yes, absolutely. I think it's something almost every actor in Mumbai goes through. Bombay mein har doosra artist kahin na kahin iss phase se guzarta hai. People often tell you, "I'm always here for you," and they genuinely mean it. But some emotions are so personal that you simply can't express them to anyone else. I'm fortunate to have good friends I can talk to whenever I need to, and I'm truly grateful for that. But there are still difficult days. Ups and downs toh har kisi ki journey ka hissa hote hain. The reality is that everyone is fighting their own battles. Your friends have their own struggles too, and you can't expect someone to carry your emotional weight forever. Har insaan ki apni journey chal rahi hoti hai. Eventually, there are questions only you can answer. The same happens in our profession. Sometimes you've invested months in a project, worked incredibly hard and built hopes around it—only for it to fall through at the last moment. People often say, "Don't have expectations." But honestly, I don't think that's practical. Jab aap itni mehnat karte ho, toh umeed toh naturally aa hi jaati hai. And when those hopes break, that's when loneliness becomes real. Many people stop expressing their emotions because they've trained themselves to stay emotionally guarded. Kabhi-kabhi hum khud ko itna strong bana lete hain ki feel karna hi kam ho jaata hai. I think that's dangerous because we're human beings, and emotions are meant to be felt, not suppressed. When I first moved from Chandigarh to Mumbai, everything was new. That's when you suddenly realise how much your family quietly does for you. At home, your mother takes care of countless little things that you never notice until you're living alone. Tab samajh aata hai ki ghar chalana kitni badi responsibility hoti hai. People often say, "Mummy toh housewife hain." But honestly, I believe mothers often work harder than anyone else in the family. That experience taught me gratitude. I'm fortunate to have people who stand beside me through both the highs and the lows. But even then, I believe everyone experiences loneliness at some point. Chahe kitne bhi log aapke saath ho, kuch safar aapko khud hi tay karne padte hain. That's why I think it's important to spend time with yourself. I'm not talking about isolation—I'm talking about self-reflection. Some questions don't have answers that anyone else can give you. You only discover those answers when you sit quietly with yourself. Thoda sa self-talk, thoda sa introspection bahut zaroori hota hai. Maybe I'm sounding a little spiritual, but that's genuinely what I believe.                    View this post on Instagram            A post shared by Ramandeep Yadav (@_ramandeep_yadav)Q: Audiences are finally noticing Rajjo. What would you like them to know about the person behind the villain? A: (Laughs) I'd like them to know that Rajjo and Raman are complete opposites. Rajjo is brutal, unpredictable and emotionally damaged. Raman, on the other hand, is a very simple, calm and cheerful person. Sach kahun toh Raman mein Rajjo ka ek per cent bhi nahi hai. One of my favourite reactions came from my sister after she watched the show. She told me, "Main show dekhne baithi thi kyunki tu tha. Lekin kuch der baad main tujhe pehchaan hi nahi paayi. Mujhe laga yeh kaun hai?" As an actor, that was probably the biggest compliment I could have received. The fact that even my own family couldn't recognise me on screen made me feel that I'd done justice to the character. Us din laga ki actor ke taur par main kahin na kahin jeet gaya. As a person, I think Raman has many different shades too, but they're very different from Rajjo's. While playing Rajjo, I poured a great deal of my emotions into him. Rajjo ko banane mein maine apne andar ki bahut saari energy aur emotions daal diye. Now that journey is over, I feel I need to refill myself emotionally before beginning the next character. That's the beauty of acting. Every role empties you a little and then fills you again in a completely different way. Going forward, I simply want to keep surprising people. I want to explore characters that are completely different from one another and continue growing as an actor. Ek hi type ke roles karke mujhe mazaa nahi aata. Main chahta hoon ki har naya character logon ko surprise kare. Hopefully, audiences will continue giving me the opportunity to show them something new every single time.

title: Rayagada: ଗ୍ରୋସେରୀ ଦୋକାନରେ ଅଗ୍ନିକାଣ୍ଡ , ଲକ୍ଷାଧିକ ଟଙ୍କାର ସାମଗ୍ରୀ ପୋଡ଼ି ପାଉଁଶ
description:
ରାୟଗଡା:ରାୟଗଡା ଜିଲ୍ଲା ଗୁଣପୁର ଥାନା ଅନ୍ତର୍ଗତ ଡମ୍ବସରା ଗ୍ରାମ ମୁଖ୍ୟ ରାସ୍ତାରେ ଥିବା ଏକ ଗ୍ରୋସରୀ ଦୋକାନରେ ଅଗ୍ନିକାଣ୍ଡ ଘଟିଛି । ଅଗ୍ନିକାଣ୍ଡର କାରଣ ସ୍ପଷ୍ଟ ହୋଇ ପାରି ନଥିବା ବେଳେ ବିଦ୍ୟୁତ ସଟ ସର୍କିଟ ଯୋଗୁଁ ଅଗ୍ନିକାଣ୍ଡ ଘଟିଥିବା ସନ୍ଦେହ କରାଯାଉଛି । ଅଗ୍ନିକାଣ୍ଡରେ ଦୋକାନର ସମସ୍ତ ସାମଗ୍ରୀ ସହ ଆସବାବ ପତ୍ର ପୋଡି ପାଉଁଶ ହୋଇଯାଇଛି । ଘଟଣା ସ୍ଥଳରେ ରାଜସ୍ବ ବିଭାଗ ପହଞ୍ଚି ସ୍ଥିତି ଅନୁଧ୍ୟାନ କରିବା ସହ କ୍ଷୟ କ୍ଷତିର ଆକଳନ କରୁଥିବା ବେଳେ ଲକ୍ଷାଧିକ ଟଙ୍କାର ଜିନିଷ ପୋଡି ନଷ୍ଟ ହୋଇଯାଇଥିବା ଜଣା ପଡିଛି ।
ଆହୁରି ପଢନ୍ତୁ: IB Chief: ମହେଶ ଦୀକ୍ଷିତ ଇଣ୍ଟେଲିଜେନ୍ସ ବ୍ୟୁରୋ ମୁଖ୍ୟ ନିଯୁକ୍ତ
ମିଳିଥିବା ସୂଚନା ଅନୁଯାୟୀ କେ ଗୁରୁନାଥ ନାମକ ଜଣେ ବ୍ୟବସାୟୀଙ୍କ ଦୋକାନରେ ଆଜି ସକାଳ ସମୟରେ ଦୋକାନ ଭିତରୁ ଧୁଆଁ ବାହାରୁଥିବା ସ୍ଥାନୀୟ ବାସିନ୍ଦା ଦେଖିବା ପରେ ବ୍ୟବସାୟୀଙ୍କୁ ଖବର ଜଣାଇଥିଲେ । ବ୍ୟବସାୟୀ ଜଣକ ଆସି ଦେଖିଲା ବେଳକୁ ନିଆଁ ସମଗ୍ର ଦୋକାନକୁ ବ୍ଯାପୀ ଯାଇଥିଲା । ଗୁଣପୁର ଅଗ୍ନିଶମ ବିଭାଗ ଘଟଣା ସ୍ଥଳରେ ପହଞ୍ଚି ନିଆଁକୁ ଆୟତ କରିଥିବା ଜଣାପଡିଛି । ପ୍ରାଥମିକ ସୂଚନାରୁ ଜଣାପଡିଛି ପ୍ରାୟ ୩୦ ଲକ୍ଷ ଟଙ୍କାରୁ ଉର୍ଧ୍ଵ ସାମଗ୍ରୀ ପୋଡି ପାଉଁଶ ହୋଇଯାଇଛି I ଗୁଣପୁର ପୁଲିସ ଏନେଇ ତଦନ୍ତ ଆରମ୍ଭ କରିଛି I

title: বৰভূঁইকপ : ছেকেণ্ডতে তচনচ হোৱা ভেনিজুৱেলাক আমেৰিকা, ব্ৰাজিলৰ সহায়ৰ হাত
CM Omar Pays Tributes to Imam Hussain (AS), Martyrs of Karbala on Youm-e-Ashura
description:
Srinagar, June 26: On the occasion of Youm-e-Ashura, Chief Minister Omar Abdullah paid heartfelt tributes to Hazrat Imam Hussain (A.S.) and his revered companions, remembering their sacrifice as a timeless example of faith, justice and humanity.
In a post on X, Office of CM wrote, On the occasion of Youm-e-Ashura, the Chief Minister paid heartfelt tributes to Imam Hussain (A.S.) and his revered companions, whose supreme sacrifice reaffirmed the timeless values of faith, justice and humanity. He said that the martyrdom of Imam Hussain (A.S.) at Karbala stands as an eternal symbol of courage, truth and unwavering commitment to justice."

IB Chief: ମହେଶ ଦୀକ୍ଷିତ ଇଣ୍ଟେଲିଜେନ୍ସ ବ୍ୟୁରୋ ମୁଖ୍ୟ ନିଯୁକ୍ତ
description:
ନୂଆଦିଲ୍ଲୀ: କେନ୍ଦ୍ର ସରକାର ଆଇପିଏସ୍‌ ଅଧିକାରୀ ମହେଶ ଦୀକ୍ଷିତଙ୍କୁ ଇଣ୍ଟେଲିଜେନ୍ସ ବ୍ୟୁରୋ (ଆଇବି)ର ପରବର୍ତ୍ତୀ ମୁଖ୍ୟ ଭାବରେ ନିଯୁକ୍ତ କରିଛନ୍ତି। ଦୀକ୍ଷିତ ତପନ ଡେକାଙ୍କ ସ୍ଥାନରେ ନୂଆ ନିଯୁକ୍ତି ପାଇଛନ୍ତି। ଜୁନ୍ ୩୦ ତାରିଖରେ ଡେକାଙ୍କ ବର୍ଦ୍ଧିତ କାର୍ଯ୍ୟକାଳ ଶେଷ ହେବା ପରେ ଦୀକ୍ଷିତ ଦାୟିତ୍ୱ ଗ୍ରହଣ କରିବେ। ହିମାଚଳ ପ୍ରଦେଶ କ୍ୟାଡରର ୧୯୮୮ବ୍ୟାଚ୍ ଅଧିକାରୀ ଡେକାଙ୍କୁ ୨୦୨୨ ରେ ଆଇବି ମୁଖ୍ୟ ଭାବରେ ନିଯୁକ୍ତ କରାଯାଇଥିଲା ଓ ତା’ପରେ ତାଙ୍କୁ ଦୁଇ ଥର ଗୋଟିଏ ବର୍ଷ ଲେଖାଏଁ ଏକ୍ସଟେନ୍ସନ୍ ଦିଆଯାଇଥିଲା।
ଆଇପିଏସ୍‌ ପୂର୍ବରୁ ଡାକ୍ତର ଥିଲେ
୧୯୯୩ ବ୍ୟାଚ ଆନ୍ଧ୍ର ପ୍ରଦେଶ କ୍ୟାଡରର ଆଇପିଏସ୍‌ ଅଧିକାରୀ ଦୀକ୍ଷିତ ବର୍ତ୍ତମାନ ଆଇବିର ସ୍ବତନ୍ତ୍ର ନିର୍ଦ୍ଦେଶକ ଭାବେ କାର୍ଯ୍ୟ କରୁଛନ୍ତି। ପ୍ରଧାନମନ୍ତ୍ରୀ ନରେନ୍ଦ୍ର ମୋଦୀଙ୍କ ଅଧ୍ୟକ୍ଷତାରେ କ୍ୟାବିନେଟର ନିଯୁକ୍ତି କମିଟି (ଏସିସି) ଦୁଇ ବର୍ଷ ପାଇଁ ଦୀକ୍ଷିତଙ୍କ ନିଯୁକ୍ତିକୁ ଅନୁମୋଦନ କରିଛି। ଦୀକ୍ଷିତ ଆଇପିଏସ୍‌ ହେବା ପୂର୍ବରୁ ଜଣେ ଡାକ୍ତର ଥିଲେ। ଆଭ୍ୟନ୍ତରୀଣ ସୁରକ୍ଷା ଚ୍ୟାଲେଞ୍ଜର ମୁକାବିଲା କରିବାରେ ତାଙ୍କର ବ୍ୟାପକ ପରିଚାଳନାଗତ ଓ ଗୁଇନ୍ଦା ଅଭିଜ୍ଞତା ରହିଛି। ତାଙ୍କର ଆତଙ୍କବାଦ ବିରୋଧୀ କାର୍ଯ୍ୟରେ ମଧ୍ୟ ଅଭିଜ୍ଞତା ରହିଛି।
ଆଇବିରେ ସ୍ୱତନ୍ତ୍ର ନିର୍ଦ୍ଦେଶକ ଭାବରେ ନିଯୁକ୍ତ ହେବା ପୂର୍ବରୁ ସେ ସମ୍ବେଦନଶୀଳ କାଶ୍ମୀର ସବସିଡିଆରୀ ଇଣ୍ଟେଲିଜେନ୍ସ ବ୍ୟୁରୋର ନେତୃତ୍ୱ ନେଇଥିଲେ ଓ ଧାରା ୩୭୦ ଉଚ୍ଛେଦ ପରେ କେନ୍ଦ୍ର ଶାସିତ ଅଞ୍ଚଳରେ ଆଇନ ଶୃଙ୍ଖଳା ବଜାୟ ରଖିବାରେ ଗୁରୁତ୍ୱପୂର୍ଣ୍ଣ ଭୂମିକା ଗ୍ରହଣ କରିଥିଲେ। ତାଙ୍କର ଆଞ୍ଚଳିକ ସୁରକ୍ଷା ବିପଦକୁ ମୁକାବିଲା କରିବାରେ ଅଭିଜ୍ଞତା ମଧ୍ୟ ଅଛି, ଉତ୍ତର-ପୂର୍ବ ଏବଂ ଲଦାଖରେ ଅପରେସନ ଠାରୁ ଆରମ୍ଭ କରି ନକ୍ସଲ ବିରୋଧୀ ଡେସ୍କ ପର୍ଯ୍ୟନ୍ତ ବିଭିନ୍ନ ବିଭାଗ ପରିଚାଳନା କରିଛନ୍ତି। ଆଇବି ଦେଶର ପ୍ରମୁଖ ଘରୋଇ ଗୁଇନ୍ଦା ସଂସ୍ଥା ଯାହା ଆତଙ୍କବାଦ, ବିଭିନ୍ନ ଅଞ୍ଚଳରେ ସଂଘର୍ଷ, ସରକାରୀ ନୀତି ସହିତ ଜଡିତ ବିପଦ ମୂଲ୍ୟାଙ୍କନ ସମେତ ଅନେକ ପ୍ରସଙ୍ଗରେ ଗୁପ୍ତ ସୂଚନା ସଂଗ୍ରହ କରେ। ଏହା ଅନ୍ୟ ଏଜେନ୍ସି ଓ ପୁଲିସ ବାହିନୀକୁ ମଧ୍ୟ ସହାୟତା ପ୍ରଦାନ କରେ।
Accident: ପିକଅପ୍ ସହ ଟ୍ରକ୍ ଧକ୍କାରେ ସାତ ଜଣଙ୍କର ମୃତ୍ୟୁ

title: KMCH Sempora expands patient-centric services with inauguration of Café, Optical Unit
Tight security arrangements in place for Youm-e-Ashura today
description:
Srinagar, June 26: Marking the martyrdom of Imam Hussain (AS) and his companions in the Battle of Karbala, Youm-e-Ashura is being observed across Kashmir today with deep religious reverence. Thousands of mourners are expected to join the main procession from Bota Kadal to Imam Bara Zadibal in Srinagar.
The procession will bring together devotees from different parts of Kashmir at Zadibal, where they will pay tribute through mourning rituals, religious recitations, Majalis and prayers.
An official said the administration has put in place elaborate arrangements to ensure the smooth conduct of the event. Healthcare facilities, drinking water, sanitation, traffic regulation and emergency response services have been arranged along the procession route.
"A multi-layer security grid has been established with deployment of police, CRPF and other security personnel, while drone surveillance and CCTV monitoring are being used to maintain round-the-clock vigilance," a senior police officer said.
The officer said the observance is being monitored closely at the highest level to ensure that Youm-e-Ashura passes off peacefully and without any untoward incident.
Lieutenant Governor Manoj Sinha is also scheduled to visit Zadibal to witness the solemn occasion and take stock of the arrangements.
In view of the expected large gathering, Srinagar Police has issued a comprehensive public advisory asking mourners and the general public to observe Muharram in a peaceful, dignified and disciplined manner.
According to the advisory, participants have been asked to maintain communal harmony, follow all directions issued by the administration and police authorities, cooperate with traffic police and volunteers for smooth movement, ensure that the procession remains confined to the permitted route and purpose, respect the religious sentiments and beliefs of all communities, and protect public and government property while maintaining cleanliness.
Police have also advised the public not to indulge in any unlawful activity or create any law and order situation, not to raise provocative slogans or make inflammatory speeches, not to obstruct traffic or pedestrian movement, and not to damage or misuse public property.
The advisory further states that people should not display provocative banners, posters, placards or flags, should not disrespect the National Flag or glorify any country, organisation or group, and should not carry or display flags or symbols of any country, organisation or group. It also cautions against engaging in any activity detrimental to the integrity, sovereignty and unity of the nation.
Srinagar Police appealed to all mourners and citizens to extend full cooperation, stating that public participation and responsible conduct would ensure a peaceful and dignified observance of Youm-e-Ashura.

ব্ৰহ্মপুত্ৰৰ খহনীয়াত সংকটত বিশ্বনাথ-পাণপুৰ মথাউৰি
description:
ডিজিটেল সংবাদ, বিশ্বনাথ : ব্ৰহ্মপুত্ৰৰ প্ৰবল গৰাখহনীয়াই সম্প্ৰতি বিশ্বনাথ ঘাট-পাণপুৰ মথাউৰিলৈ ভাবুকি কঢ়িয়াই আনিছে। বিশ্বনাথৰ দক্ষিণাঞ্চলৰ ৰক্ষাকৱচ স্বৰূপ এই মথাউৰিটোৰ বিস্তৰ ক্ষতিৰ আশংকা কৰা হৈছে।
শিঙিমাৰী-কালাকাটি অংশত অব্যাহত থকা খহনীয়াৰ ফলত মথাউৰিৰ পৰা ব্ৰহ্মপুত্ৰৰ দূৰত্ব মাত্ৰ ১৫-২০ মিটাৰলৈ সংকুচিত হৈছে। স্থানীয় ৰাইজৰ আশংকা, এনেদৰে খহনীয়া চলি থাকিলে কেইদিনমানৰ ভিতৰতে মথাউৰি চিগি সমগ্ৰ অঞ্চল প্লাৱিত হ’ব পাৰে। ব্ৰহ্মপুত্ৰৰ এই আগ্ৰাসী ৰূপে নৈপৰীয়া ৰাইজৰ নিদ্ৰাহৰণ কৰিছে।

পৰিস্থিতিৰ গুৰুত্ব সাপেক্ষে বিশ্বনাথৰ জিলা দণ্ডাধীশ লক্ষীনন্দন চহৰীয়াই ভাৰতীয় নাগৰিক সুৰক্ষা সংহিতা, ২০২৩ৰ ধাৰা ১৬৩ ৰ অধীনত কেতবোৰ নিষেধাজ্ঞা জাৰি কৰিছে। মথাউৰিৰ সংলগ্ন দুৰ্বল অংশ আৰু খহনীয়া-প্ৰৱণ অঞ্চলত সকলো ৰাজহুৱা, ধৰ্মীয়, সাংস্কৃতিক সভা, মেলা সম্পূৰ্ণৰূপে নিষিদ্ধ।

খহনীয়া প্ৰভাৱিত অঞ্চল আৰু মথাউৰিৰ দুৰ্বল অংশত সাধাৰণ জনতাৰ প্ৰৱেশ নিষিদ্ধ। কেৱল স্থানীয় বাসিন্দা আৰু জৰুৰীকালীন, সাহায্য, উদ্ধাৰ বা ৰক্ষণাবেক্ষণৰ কামত নিয়োজিত বিষয়াকহে অনুমতি দিয়া হ’ব। 

জিলা প্ৰশাসনৰ পূৰ্বৰ লিখিত অনুমতি অবিহনে ক্ষতিগ্ৰস্ত অঞ্চলত কোনো অনুষ্ঠানৰ আয়োজন কৰিব নোৱাৰিব। যোৱা শতিকাৰ শেষৰ ফালৰ পৰাই এই মথাউৰি খহনীয়াত আক্ৰান্ত হৈ আহিছে।ইতিমধ্যে জলসম্পদ বিভাগে জিঅ’ বেগৰ জৰিয়তে খহনীয়া নিয়ন্ত্ৰণৰ প্ৰচেষ্টা চলাইছে যদিও মথাউৰিটো ৰক্ষা কৰিব পৰা যাব নে নাযায় সেয়া এতিয়া লাখ টকীয়া প্ৰশ্ন হৈ পৰিছে।

The Battle for Sullivan’s Island
description: Marshy, sandy terrain and an impassable inlet helped colonial forces repel British forces during a pivotal battle on the barrier island near Charleston, South Carolina, on June 28, 1776.
content:
Earth Observatory

The Battle for Sullivan’s…

Signs of the marshy, sandy terrain that helped colonists repel invading British forces in a pivotal battle in June 1776 remain visible on Sullivan’s Island in this image acquired on June 3, 2026, by the OLI (Operational Land Imager) on Landsat 8.
NASA Earth Observatory/Michala Garrison



As Thomas Jefferson and the Committee of Five presented their first draft of the Declaration of Independence in Philadelphia on June 28, 1776, several British warships and thousands of troops were massing around Sullivan’s Island in South Carolina.
The pitched battle for the sandy barrier island at the mouth of Charleston Harbor that played out over the course of that June day was one of the most significant in the early stages of the Revolutionary War. By nightfall, largely untested colonial troops had decisively defeated the British, an outcome that helped save Charleston from occupation and buoyed American spirits at a critical stage of the war.
The Landsat 8 satellite captured this image of the island on June 3, 2026. Two hundred fifty years earlier, the sandy beaches, salt marshes, and general shape of the island would have looked similar, though with less evidence of roads or other signs of human development.
There certainly would have been some signs of human activity on the island, however. Quite noticeable would have been Fort Sullivan, a large square structure built from palmetto logs on the southern tip of the island, near the entrance to the harbor. Though one side of the fort, assembled largely by enslaved people, was still unfinished at the time of the battle, the other sides had 16-foot-wide walls packed with sand and containing planked gun platforms that mounted 31 cannons.
Historical maps show at least one road extending from the southern to northern tip of Sullivan’s Island, where hundreds of colonial soldiers were also encamped to protect Breach Inlet from a force of roughly 3,000 British troops massing on nearby Long Island (now Isle of Palms). When the battle began, historians estimate that there were roughly 800 colonial troops, including dozens of Catawba warriors, defending the northeastern part of Sullivan’s Island, embedded within earthen defenses and manning two artillery pieces.




June 3, 2026



When the British attack came on the morning of June 28, 1776, both military tactics and geography played critical roles in determining the outcome. Having been told the water at the inlet was less than 18 inches (46 centimeters) deep at low tide, the British commander had planned to have his forces walk across Breach Inlet on foot. But he was forced to pivot to a more dangerous amphibious assault using flatboats when he realized the shallowest part of the break was at least 7 feet (2 meters) deep at low tide. Traveling by flatboat limited the number of British troops who could cross the channel at once, making it easier for colonial defenders to repel them during fierce skirmishing throughout the day.
On the other side of the island, British warships had dropped anchor near Fort Sullivan and begun launching thousands of cannonballs and exploding shells at the fort. However, the natural durability and pliability of the palmetto wood absorbed incoming fire “like sponges,” Colonel William Moultrie, the fort’s commanding officer, later noted in his memoirs.
Most incoming shells that fell within the fort’s walls were neutralized. There was a marshy “morass” in the center of the fort, Moultrie wrote, that “swallowed” up incoming fire “instantly.” Shells that made it over the walls and “fell in the sand, in and about the fort, were immediately buried, so that few of them burst amongst us,” he wrote.
With their limited powder, the colonists focused their fire on the ship carrying the British commander, Sir Peter Parker, severely damaging it and ultimately killing 40 people on board. By the evening, exhausted from the 10-hour battle and making little progress, the British forces retreated.
“We never had such a drubbing in our lives,” one Royal Navy sailor wrote. After the battle, the fort became known as Fort Moultrie, and the palmetto tree began appearing on the state seal in what would prove to be an enduring symbol of colonial pride and resistance. Six days after the battle, the Declaration of Independence was adopted in Philadelphia.

NASA Earth Observatory images by Michala Garrison, using Landsat data from the U.S. Geological Survey. Story by Adam Voiland.

title: Hyundai exits family hatch game with no plan to replace i30
description:
"The business case is not extremely compelling," says Hyundai boss about the prospect of a new i30

Hyundai is set to retire the i30 hatchback with no direct successor as demand for traditional petrol hatchbacks continues to fall in Europe.
The i30 has been off sale in the UK for a year while remaining available in some European markets, but it will soon be axed for good – and any replacement is set to take a different form.
Asked about plans for the i30's future – following the unveiling of a new-generation i20 supermini earlier this month, and the launch of the similarly sized Ioniq 3 EV – Hyundai's Europe CEO Xavier Martinet hinted that no fourth-generation i30 is in the works.
"There might be a reason why we don't talk much about it," he said of the company's petrol-powered Volkswagen Golf rival. 
"The problem is that in this segment the demand is not growing, and it's also a vehicle that historically was mostly a fleet vehicle, on which the profit is not so high."
The small proportion of retail sales was not enough to compensate for the low-profit fleet sales, he added, because "the price was lower, so the profits [were as well].
"When you put everything together, the business case is not extremely compelling."

In recent years, several of the i30's closest rivals have also been cancelled, including the Ford Focus, Kia Ceed and Renault Megane. 
Strong-selling stalwarts including the Volkswagen Golf, Peugeot 308 and Toyota Corolla continue, but the segment accounts for far fewer European sales than it once did, with mid-sized crossovers becoming the default choice as practical family runarounds.
Opel-Vauxhall boss Florian Huettl voiced similar sentiments to Martinet recently when he hinted at plans to replace the i30's Astra rival with a less "traditional" C-segment offering – potentially some sort of SUV.
Hyundai could take a similar route as it looks to fill the gap left by the i30, Martinet suggested: "We might still have some answers coming, but it might be a different answer to the one we've got so far.
"I might be a bit cryptic in my answer, but at some point in time we have to know when to stop and how to do the next generation – this is one of the question marks we have."

title: Toyota Aygo X used buying guide: The most reliable £6000 car?
description:
Tiny crossover is a brilliantly frugal city car, even without hybrid power

If you're after a tiny, frugal, practical city runaround, your choices on the new car market are currently quite limited.
Razor-thin margins in this segment and increasingly costly regulations sent most manufacturers hurtling towards the exit door a few years back, leaving only a few faithful defenders to choose from today.
Toyota is one of these holdouts, and its Aygo X happens to be among the best of the breed - but if you haven't bought a new car in a while, the new hybrid model's £21,500 price might come as a bit of a shock.
Happily, you can pick up a four-year-old pure-petrol model for as little as £6500. These pre-facelift cars are still capable of 60mpg on a run, and most have a snickety five-speed manual gearbox that makes them surprisingly fun to punt around.

When the Aygo became the Aygo X in 2022, it moved onto an entirely new platform and was repositioned as a sort of pocket-sized SUV: still dinky but larger in all dimensions than before, with a raised seating position and the requisite lower body cladding.
So it's slightly longer and wider than conventionally styled rivals like the Kia Picanto and Hyundai i10 and therefore more spacious inside, yet it remains easy to park and thread along country lanes and tight city streets (with a 9.4m turning circle that's one of the tightest this side of a black cab). Plus, because you sit a bit higher, you don't feel like you're so vulnerable when hemmed in by buses or trucks.
The Aygo X weighs less than a tonne, too, which means that its 71bhp 1.0-litre atmospheric three-pot doesn't feel especially minuscule in everyday driving, and its tiny 35-litre fuel tank can still hold enough for 450 miles if you go easy. You do have to work it quite hard to keep up with traffic (0-62mph takes about 15sec), but it's at least quite good fun to rev out with the manual. The optional CVT drones on but is smooth. Further benefits of the Aygo X's trim kerb weight include genuinely fun handling, although the ride can get a bit choppy over rough stretches of road.

Space up front is good, and the seats provide plenty of support for longer drives. Adults will be fine in the back for a bit, so long as they're not especially tall and you don't have the head room-robbing canvas roof option - and kids will be fine either way (if maybe miffed that their windows only pop out).
In basic Pure trim it lacks any real visual clout, but step up to mid-spec Edge (as most buyers did) and you gain 18in diamond-cut alloys and eye-catching two-tone metallic paint. Exclusive trim adds desirable LED headlights for a smarter and more contemporary vibe.
All versions get wireless Apple CarPlay and Android Auto - on a 7.0in, 8.0in or 9.2in infotainment touchscreen, depending on spec - plus adaptive cruise control, automatic wipers, automatic lights, automatic high beam and a reversing camera.
Edge trim brings climate control, privacy glass and front foglights, while Exclusive adds a wireless phone charger, keyless go and parking sensors front and rear.

The Aygo X is a reassuringly traditional small car that still manages to feel modern and capable enough to rival its newer alternatives - scant few as there may be - while retaining a touch of charm and panache that makes it easy to love.
What to look out for
Warranty: Toyota has a great reputation for reliability and - as you will see in this section - the Aygo X conforms to type, with an exemplary record in reliability surveys. Almost all used examples will still be covered by the standard warranty (five years or 100,000 miles), which you can extend a year at a time by servicing the car at a Toyota main dealer (up to 10 years old).
Engine: The 1.0-litre VVT-i engine has been powering Aygos for more than two decades and is known to be very reliable. That doesn't mean you shouldn't check the oil before buying, however, and be wary of any cars without a complete service history.
Body: This is a city car, so check for kerb damage to the alloy wheels and scuffs around the body.
Insurance: Like many of its small car classmates, the Aygo is among the cheapest cars to insure, ranging between groups five and seven. Our quote of £307 for a year was for a 35-year-old professional male with a clean licence and full no-claims bonus living in Swindon.
Also worth knowing
Special editions are numerous, although none received chassis or powertrain alterations. The Limited Edition added Army Green paint and orange highlights, quilted leather heated seats and the canvas roof. The Undercover Edition, created in collaboration with a Japanese fashion designer, had blueish-grey paint and red accents, plus 'Chaos' and 'Balance' logos on the roof and the floor mats. Its red seats are a bold touch.
The JBL Special Edition gets a premium sound system from JBL, which was otherwise an option. It's worth seeking out if you're a music lover. There's even a subwoofer under the boot floor.
Air Edition was based on Edge trim (the others used Exclusive as a base) but had the canvas roof as standard and came in a few more colours, such as Brass Gold. Annual VED is £195 on all cars.
How much to spend
£6500-£8499 A few 100,000-milers, which is a testament to Aygo X reliability. Also plenty of write-offs, many with a tenth of that mileage and in top trims. Check the severity of the damage before you buy.
£8500-£10,999 Mostly early Pure models with average mileage for their age (40,000-50,000), although a few Edge versions stand out.
£11,000-£12,999 Plenty of Edges and Exclusives, some with the CVT gearbox or the roll-back canvas roof.
£13,000-£20,000 Lightly used special editions, nearly new cars and even a few unregistered examples with delivery mileages.
An owner's view
James Smyth "I bought my Aygo X Edge in 2022 as a runabout for the city, and it has been brilliant. It looks fantastic - much more premium than other small cars - and the high driving position gives great visibility. It's surprisingly comfortable on longer trips too, although the engine does need to be worked hard on motorways. The infotainment is a big step up from my old Aygo. And fuel economy is excellent: I regularly see 60mpg without trying. Servicing has been cheap and reliability faultless so far. It's a small car that doesn't feel cheap, which is exactly what I wanted."

Government Hikes Passport Fees—Here's How Much You Will Pay From July 1
description: The government has announced an increase in passport application fees, effective July 1 through amendments to the Passports Rules, 1980.
content: The Centre has increased passport application fees by amending the Passports Rules, 1980, with the cost of a fresh 36-page ordinary passport rising from Rs 1,500 to Rs 2,500. The revised fee structure, notified by the Ministry of External Affairs, will come into effect from July 1, 2026, and also raises Tatkal charges for the same category to Rs 5,000.At present, the application fee for an ordinary fresh passport or reissue of a passport containing 36 pages stands at Rs 1,500, and for tatkal it is Rs 3,500 for the same category.Similarly, the application fee for an ordinary fresh passport or reissue of a passport containing 60 pages has been hiked from Rs 2,000 to Rs 3,500 and for tatkal, the revised fee will be Rs 6,000 as against Rs 4,000 for the same category."In exercise of the powers conferred by section 24 of the Passports Act, 1967 (15 of 1967), the Central Government hereby makes the following rules further to amend the Passports Rules, 1980, namely -- 1. (1) These rules may be called the Passports (Amendment) Rules, 2026. (2) They shall come into force with effect from the 1st day of July, 2026," the notification said.Also Read: If Passport Is Not Proof Of Citizenship, Then What Is?In its notification, the government also issued a Schedule which will substitute the "Schedule IV to the Passports Rules, 1980".The revised schedule mentions two sub-categories -- for applicants (18 years of age and above/minors aged between 15 to 18 years, if applied under this category); and minor applicants (below 18 years of age).The developments come after the Ministry of External Affairs asserted, on Passport Seva Divas, that an Indian passport is a travel document and "not a document of citizenship." MEA's clarification reignited a debate over what legally establishes Indian citizenship.
